abstract:
It is widely recognized that training deep neural networks effectively requires massive datasets comprising thousands of annotated samples. In this paper, we present "U-Net," a novel network architecture and training strategy specifically designed to work efficiently with limited data through the heavy use of data augmentation. The architecture features a symmetric design: a contracting path to capture global context and an expanding path that enables precise, pixel-level localization. 

We demonstrate that U-Net can be trained end-to-end using very few images while still outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods—such as sliding-window convolutional networks—on the ISBI challenge for segmenting neuronal structures in electron microscopy stacks. Furthermore, our network achieved a decisive victory in the 2015 ISBI Cell Tracking Challenge for transmitted light microscopy categories (phase contrast and DIC) by a significant margin. Beyond its accuracy, the network is highly efficient; a single 512x512 image can be segmented in less than a second on a modern GPU. We have made our full Caffe-based implementation and pre-trained networks available at: http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net.
